observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the City Accounting Office to:

Make the necessary adjustments, when necessary and upon receipt of information and result of verification from the concerned AGDBs, in compliance with Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 3.1, 3.2 and 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 so as to present fairly the balance of the Cash in Bank - LCCA account in the Statement of Financial Position as of year-end; and

Review and adjust the book error amounting to ₱690,000.00 which remained a reconciling item despite recording by the bank in CY 2022; elsewise, to exert earnest efforts to locate the source documents and examine the same for proper recording.
observations.managementAction:
The CAO has coordinated with the concerned AGDB/s through the CTO and has requested details and particulars of the various reconciling items.

Details of some reconciling items have been provided by the bank and recorded by the CAO in the City's books. Adjusting entries have been made in the current year.

However, there are still some items that lack details as a basis for adjustment. The CAO is exerting its best efforts to analyze the records, including a book error amounting to ₱690,000.00.
observations.reason: On-going verification of the source documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

through the Inventory Committee, complete the conduct of physical inventory of the City’s PPE and prepare and submit the corresponding report therefrom in strict compliance to Section 113 of Section 226 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs;

through the City Accounting Office, undertake continuous reconciliation on the result of the physical count with the property and accounting records and adjust the book of accounts and the property records, as warranted, to reflect the correct balances of the affected PPE accounts in order to fairly present the PPE accounts in the financial statements as of year-end; and

instruct the concerned offices to study the applicability of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 which provides the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE Items found at station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items, for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of government agencies to facilitate fair presentation of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Memorandum Order No. 329-2024 was issued to all Department and Unit Heads, directing them to designate one (1) Property Officer/ Custodian and one (1) Logistics Officer in their respective offices to conduct the initial physical inventory of all PPEs.
observations.reason: Ongoing reconciliation of PPE records between the Accounting Office and the Property Unit of the GSO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Require the concerned departments to exert necessary efforts to locate all required documents for the titling of 18 sites which were acquired through donation or purchase by the City in order to establish its ownership over the subject lands and to protect the interest of the City; and

Constitute the Inventory Committee tasked to determine the value of the 37 lots with a total area of 193,313 square meters already titled in the name of the City pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to provide basis in recording the same in the books of accounts of the City by the City Accounting Office, to facilitate the fair presentation of the Land account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City is undertaking efforts to secure the documents necessary for the processing of land titles.

The CTO has implemented the necessary measures to enforce the remittance of collections from CTCs and RPTs. The CTO committed to strictly enforce the same prospectively. However, there were instances when collections from Accountable Form 51 were not remitted in a timely manner.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management, through the City Accounting Office, further analyze the unremitted balances in the total amount of ₱5.625 million in order to come-up with the details/composition thereof as basis in remitting to the concerned agencies in compliance with applicable rules and regulations.

Moreover, we recommended that the Management, through the City Accounting Office, continue exerting earnest efforts to determine the cause of the negative balances in the aforementioned payable accounts and make the necessary adjustments or corrections, whenever necessary.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office conducts a monthly review of the subsidiary ledger balances to check for any abnormal balances during the month that may require immediate adjustment or correction.
observations.reason: Due to a lack of manpower and voluminous daily transactions, the accounting office finds it challenging to promptly meet compliance requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management enforce the settlement of disallowances in the amount of ₱1.524 million in strict compliance to Sections 5.4, 7.1.1 and 7.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2009 – 001 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that the Management create a composite team to work on the details/composition of the roads, highways and bridges previously recorded in the RPI which were already transferred to the Road Networks account in lump sum in the amount of ₱390.861 million as of December 31, 2023, to ensure that the booked amount reflects the correct value of the road networks. 

Likewise, we recommended that the Management conduct physical count of road networks and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCLRN and update and maintain the Local Road Inventory Report and Road Map to ensure complete and accurate records of Road Network pursuant to Items VI (3) and VII (b) (1) of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.
observations.reason: Due to a lack of manpower and voluminous daily transactions, the accounting office finds it challenging to promptly meet compliance requirements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management, through the City Accounting Office (CAO), take immediate action to analyze all available documents and promptly adjust the reconciling items with sufficient supporting details, to ensure the accurate and fair presentation of the Cash in Bank – LCCA in the Statement of Financial Position, in compliance with Section 74 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and Sections 3.1, 3.2, and 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management exert earnest efforts to locate, verify, and retrieve supporting documents and historical records from prior years for long-outstanding reconciling items to facilitate the proper reconciliation of accounts.

We recommended that the Management, through the CAO, provide the proper depreciation of its assets by conducting a comprehensive review of the lapsing schedules of the City's other PPE components to identify and correct any similar inconsistencies in depreciation to facilitate fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management, through the CAO, reflect the proper classification of its account by conducting a comprehensive review of the schedules of the City's other PPE components to identify and correct any similar inconsistencies in asset classification.

We recommended that Management, through all concerned offices, ensure the fair presentation of expenses in its financial statements by:
requiring all offices to use appropriate expense accounts in their budget proposals, ensuring that budget submissions align with government accounting and budgeting standards and accurately reflect the nature and purpose of the expenses, thereby preventing misclassification at the budgeting stage;
strengthening the review process of budget proposals by the CBO through careful evaluation of the accounts used in the proposals, ensuring they correspond to the correct expense classifications before finalizing the budget; and
ensuring that the CAO records actual transactions under the proper expense accounts, accurately reflecting their true nature and purpose.

We recommended that Management, through the CAO, comply with the provisions of Sections 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 by:
preparing the necessary adjustment for the reversal of the CY 2017 unspent LDRRMF to the unappropriated surplus of the GF; and
promptly recognizing the transfer of unspent balances of the DRRMF for QRF and MOOE under the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account or whenever actual transfer is not possible as at year-end, transfers could be alternatively recorded through the “Due to Other Funds” and “Due from Other Funds” accounts.

We recommended that Management continue its efforts in enforcing settlements of cash advances and other receivables, and where collection remains unlikely or is no longer feasible, take proactive measures to ensure the fair presentation of these accounts in the City’s financial statements by:
assessing the impairment of outstanding receivables and recognizing an impairment loss or allowance for probable credit losses as prescribed by IPSAS 41; and
requesting the write-off of dormant accounts in accordance with the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2023-008.

We recommended the Management, through the BAC, to properly conduct bid evaluation and post-qualification proceedings in order to validate submitted documents and representations of the winning bidder, including the NFCC, in faithful compliance to relevant provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and in order to uphold integrity of the procurement processes of the City.

We recommended the Management, through the BAC and other City Offices, to ensure that all infrastructure projects undertaken by the City are supported by duly approved CSHP in strict compliance with Section 5 of DOLE Department Order No. 13 series of 1998 Section 37.2.3 (f) of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in order to protect the interest of the City.

We recommended that Management, through the BAC, ensure the accurate preparation of all bidding documents, particularly the BOQ, in accordance with the approved POW, by instituting a thorough review and validation process prior to advertisement to uphold the integrity and fairness of the procurement process.

We recommended that the Management, through the BAC, ensure strict compliance with Section 39 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to protect the interest of the City by:
requiring the contractors for Projects PB-INF-2019-057 and PB-INF-2023-046 to submit the extended Performance Security Bonds to cover approved contract time extensions; and
ensure the posting of performance securities from winning bidders prior to the signing of contracts in strict compliance with Section 39.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and to protect the interest of the City.

We recommended that Management, through the CAO, take immediate steps to strictly enforce the posting of warranty securities by suppliers and contractors in the procurement of goods and infrastructure assets, in adherence to Sections 62.1 and 62.2.3.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended the Management, through the CAO and CEO, the following:
compile and promptly submit the necessary documentary requirements supporting the approved variation orders for Project PB-INF-2019-057, including those outlined in this report;
ensure that all variation orders (VOs) are fully supported by the required documentation as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001, promoting transparency and accountability and ensuring that all changes are justifiable and properly documented; and
observe the allowable limit of variation orders prescribed in Sections 1.1 and 1.4, Annex E of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that Management, through the BAC, strictly comply with the SLCC requirement to ensure that prospective bidders possess adequate experience in handling projects of comparable nature and scale in compliance with the requirements of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management, through the concerned offices, ensure strict compliance with Section 4, Annex E, of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, by:
strictly monitoring the advance payments made to contractors, and ensuring that all advances are supported by the required securities and systematically recouped through progress payments; and
taking prompt action to resolve the long-outstanding unrecouped advances by thoroughly reviewing contracts, verifying the securities submitted, confirming the actual worked accomplished, and assessing whether full recoupment is still feasible, without prejudice to the imposition of any other legal actions necessary for their settlement.

We recommended that the Management, through the CEO and CAO, undertake the following actions to rectify the deficiencies observed:
require the contractor to increase the amount of the Bond to cover the entire amount of the revised contract cost, to ensure continued financial protection until the completion of the defects liability period; and
for future contracts, strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 6, Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 by applying the 10 percent retention on all succeeding progress payments.

We recommended that the Management, through the BAC, enhance compliance with the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 by consistently inviting qualified observers to all procurement stages, including bid evaluation and post-qualification, in accordance with Section 13 of the same IRR.

We recommended that Management, through the offices concerned, ensure strict compliance with the withholding and remittance of taxes, mandatory government deductions and contributions by:
promptly remitting intact all withheld funds and contributions during the month to the appropriate government agencies by the ensuing month, in faithful compliance with Section 69 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and other applicable laws, rules, and regulations; and
sustaining ongoing efforts to analyze identified negative balances, further verifying their nature and cause, and making the necessary adjustments as needed to resolve any discrepancies.

We recommended that the Management, through the CEO, undertake the following actions to address the issues in the reconstruction of the Tuguegarao City Public Market (Don Domingo) and safeguard the interests of the City:
enforce a strict timeline for the final completion of the unfinished works identified during the preliminary inspection (punch list) for Project PB-INF-2019-057, and ensure that any remaining deficiencies are promptly addressed in compliance with project standards to prevent further delays;
evaluate whether liquidated damages should be imposed on the contractor for failing to complete the project within the agreed duration, including extensions and deduct the same from outstanding payments, to the Contractor; and
submit the other required reports and documents to the Audit Team to accurately assess the current status of the project and to facilitate validation and inspection.

We commended the Management and the concerned Departments for the efficient and effective planning for the timely implementation of PPAs resulting in the full implementation of 61 PPAs, equivalent to 80.75 percent of the total 20% DF appropriations for CY 2024, in accordance with DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.
Nevertheless, we recommended that Management, prioritize the implementation of the remaining PPAs to optimally utilize the available funds and ensure the achievement of desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City, in compliance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that the Management, through the City Accounting Office, review and recompute the provision of accumulated depreciation in the affected PPEs and make necessary adjustments in pursuance with IPSAS 17, and to fairly present the account balances of PPE and other related accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office recomputed the accumulated depreciation as of December 31, 2023, for the affected PPEs and made the necessary adjustments by debiting the Prior Years' Adjustment account and crediting the Accumulated Depreciation account. Thereafter, the recording of monthly depreciation expenses is properly reviewed and reconciled with the auto-generated amounts from the eNGAS before posting to the respective subsidiary and/or property ledgers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management, whenever practicable, 

Require the submission of liquidation reports, together with a copy of the journal entry voucher taking up the subject expenditures, from the concerned implementing agencies; and 

Refrain from providing additional financial assistance when the liquidation reports for previous grants were not yet submitted; in faithful adherence to Item 5 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 111(1) of PD No. 1445.

We further recommended that Management instruct the City Accounting Office to study the applicability of COA Circular No. 2023 – 008 dated August 17, 2023 which prescribes the guidelines and procedures to be observed for the proper disposition of dormant accounts.

observations.managementAction: The Management, through the concerned departments, complied with the recommendation, except for the preparation of the Program of Works (POW) for the utilization of the funds of the Office of the Building Official (OBO).
observations.reason: The City Engineer, designated as the Building Official, shall coordinate with the proper government agencies for training in the preparation of the POW and other matters related to the utilization of the OBO’s funds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Strictly monitor the grant and enforce the settlement and liquidation of cash advances provided to officers and employees in strict compliance to applicable provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;

When necessary, opt to withhold the salaries of officials and employees who failed to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of the same Circular; and

Through the City Legal Office, follow through with the issuance of demand letters to the concerned officials and employees or avail of other legal remedies to enforce the settlement or liquidation of cash advances granted.
observations.managementAction: Ongoing collaboration between the City Legal Office and the City Accounting Office, with the furnishing of an updated Schedule of Unliquidated Cash Advances as a basis for the issuance of notices to the concerned city officials and employees.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the concerned NGO and the City Accounting Office or the focal persons/unit in charge of the project to submit the aforementioned lacking documentary requirements in conformity with Item 2.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, to enable the Audit Team to conduct proper evaluation of the said transaction.

We also recommended that Management strictly monitor the implementation of the projects and programs sourced from the said fund transfers and require the NGO to liquidate the same in compliance with Item 2.2 of the same Circular.

We recommended the Management, through the City Accounting Office and City Treasurer’s Office to cause the return of the unused balance of the fund transfers from various NGAs in compliance with Item 6.7 of COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and to record proper adjusting entries to facilitate fair presentation of the financial statements.

We further recommended that Management ensure the proper safekeeping of every MOA that the City will undertake in order to provide a proper basis for its monitoring of projects, programs and activities and to monitor compliance to various terms and condition agreed upon by the parties, including those pertaining to unutilized/unspent fund transfers.

We recommended that the Management:

Cease the grant of hazard pay to officials and employees who are no longer assigned in an establishment specifically mentioned in Section 21 of R.A. No. 7305 and are not principally engaged in the delivery of health or health-related services; and

Require the concerned official to refund the hazard pay granted to her from July 2022 to June 2023 amounting to P0.113 million.

We recommended that the BAC carefully perform detailed evaluation and post-qualification of eligible bids to ensure that computational errors and omissions are considered before the execution of the contract between the City and the winning bidder.

Likewise, we recommended that Management enforce the refund of the excess of the re-calculated bid price over the submitted financial bid proposal of the contractor amounting to P32,130.00, to protect the interest of the City.

We recommended that the BAC, moving forward, exercise diligence in the preparation and execution of contract agreements to prevent possible errors that may raise questions as to the validity and propriety of procurement documents and the processes they represent. 

It is likewise recommended that the BAC ensure that the work duration stipulated in the contract is consistent with those stated in its accompanying documents in order to provide a valid basis for the monitoring of the City’s various projects and to properly determine delays and the resulting liquidated damages provided for under Section 8.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the BAC ensure that a representative of a duly recognized private group and an NGO be invited, in addition to representatives from the Commission on Audit, to sit as observers in the proceedings for all stages of procurement of the City’s various programs and projects, including the bid evaluation and post-qualification stages in faithful compliance to Section 13.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Management instruct the BAC to comply with the requirement on the submission of APPs, PMRs, and APCPI Results to the GPPB pursuant to applicable provisions of GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020, in order to enable the City to achieve its performance targets and to avert any risk of possible sanctions due to such non-compliance.

We recommended that the Management, through the City Planning and Development Officer, judicially plan and evaluate the City’s PPAs for each budget year to properly identify priority development projects that can be implemented during the year through efficient planning and procurement activities to mitigate, if not totally prevent, delays in the implementation of projects pursuant to Section 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 and Item 5 of DILG-DBM JMC No 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011.

Moreover, we recommended that the Management expedite the implementation of all unimplemented PPAs identified herein to optimize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund as mandated under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
observations.reason: The City is in the process of negotiating with the prospective seller for the acquisition of the sanitary landfill.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management, through the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer, ensure that the PPAs for each budget year funded out of the 5% LDDRMF are well planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready to mitigate, if not totally prevent, delays in the implementation of projects to fully achieve the desired goals and objectives in reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the City pursuant to RA No. 10121.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accounting Office to assign specific personnel who shall be primarily responsible to conduct a thorough analysis of the subject payable accounts to determine the root cause of the negative balances, reconciling items and unaccounted transition balances in the total amount of P30,086,138.30, P298,9004.68 and P6,341,058.12 respectively.
observations.managementAction:
Some accounts with verified negative balances were deducted from the Terminal Leave Benefits of employees who have resigned / retired during the year.

Negative balances resulting from mis-postings were already corrected by drawing the necessary adjusting entries. The corresponding JEVs were submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Since the negative/ abnormal balances may be due to transactions which transpired several years ago, it will take a significant amount of time for the City Accounting Office to identify the causes and initiate the necessary corrective measures.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The City Accounting Office draw the necessary adjusting entries to appropriately recognize the advance payments of Real Property Tax collections for CY 2023 collected in CY 2022 amounting to Php 1,891,531.41; and

The City Treasury Office shall prepare and submit in a separate report all advance receipts of taxes from taxpayers to the City Accounting Office to facilitate proper accounting thereof in accordance with IPSAS 23 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2023, the City Treasurer’s Office already submitted the list of advance payments made by taxpayers to the City Accounting Office for recording purposes.
observations.reason: The Audit Team has not received a copy of the JEV showing that the necessary adjusting entries have been taken up by the City Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:

Require the concerned departments to exert necessary efforts to locate all required documents for the titling of 18 sites which were acquired through donation or purchase by the city; and

Direct the     Inventory Committee to        determine the value of the 37 lots with a total area of 193,313 square meters already title in the name of the city pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 200 in order to provide basis in recording the same in the books of account of the city by the City Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction:
Necessary documents to facilitate the processing of land titles are currently being located by Management.




The Inventory Committee has not convened to discuss and determine the fair market values of the subject 37 lots, which will be the basis in the recording of the City Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor create a task force committee that can responsibly and knowledgeably do the conduct of One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances as provided in the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. If possible, assign three to five personnel from each division/office as members of the committee in order to facilitate the counting and determination of whereabouts of items located in their office. The Property Office in coordination with the committee shall be responsible in the overall preparation and submission of the required Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment as of December 31, 2022 and every year thereafter.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accounting Office and the City General Services Office are in the process of reconciling their respective list of properties and equipment.

As agreed between the said offices, physical count will be conducted in the 1st quarter of CY 2024.
observations.reason:
The City Accounting Office and the City General Services Office are in the process of reconciling their respective list of properties and equipment.

As agreed between the said offices, physical count will be conducted in the 1st quarter of CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor create a team to work on the details/ composition of the roads, highways and bridges previously recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure (RPI) which were already transferred to the Road Networks account in lump sum amount of P355,720,730.21 as of December 31, 2022, to ensure that the booked amount reflects the correct value of the road networks.

Likewise, conduct physical count of Road Networks and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCLRN and update and maintain the Local Road Inventory Report and Road Map to ensure complete and accurate records of Road Network pursuant to Items VI (3) and VII (b) (1) of COA Circular No. 2015.
observations.reason: While the City Planning and Development Coordinator mentioned that she tried to open the matter to facilitate the preparation of the required documents, the Management has not initiated any action regarding the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accounting Office to prepare a list/schedule of PPE to be transferred to the City Schools Division Office / Department of Education based on the existing subsidiary ledgers of the PPE accounts.

Thereafter, create an inventory team composed of City General Services Office and City Accounting Office, if possible, with a representative from the Schools Division Office/DepEd to conduct physical count to determine their existence as basis for the turnover of the PPE.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office have initially prepared the list of PPE based on the subsidiary ledgers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to withhold the salaries of concerned officials and employees who failed to liquidate their cash advances at year-end pursuant to items 5.1.3 and 5.8 of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. Likewise, require the officials and employees with outstanding cash advances who were separated from the service to execute Affidavit of Undertaking that their outstanding cash advances will be paid upon receipt of their terminal leave pay.

We further recommended that the City Mayor adopt strict measures to regulate the granting of cash advances in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office requested the existing officers and employees to liquidate their cash advances thru submission of liquidation reports and/or refund thru salary deduction.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2023, the outstanding balance of cash advances granted to officers and employees amounted to ₱1,828,950.87.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accounting Office to assign one personnel to take charge in the preparation of the required documents for the request for write-off of dormant accounts in the total amount of P4,780,437.74 in accordance with applicable provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and immediately submit the same to COA for evaluation
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office is in the process of reconciling the Subsidiary Ledger balances of the Other Receivables account.  Subsequently, the necessary documents for the write-off of dormant accounts shall be prepared.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accounting Office analyze the unremitted balances in the total amount of P17,128,484.85 in order to come up with the details/composition thereof as basis in remitting to the concerned agencies.


Henceforth, all amount withheld during the month should be immediately remitted in the ensuing month in faithful adherence to Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and other applicable rules and regulations. It should be emphasized that any penalty imposed against the City shall be the personal liability of the agency withholding agent.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office makes it a point to prevent delays in the remittance of mandatory deductions to the concerned agencies.
observations.reason: Due to numerous subsidiary ledger account maintained and lack of personnel, it will take some time for the Accounting Office to   analyze the unremitted balance. Nonetheless, the Accounting Office commit to look into these and come up with a detailed composition.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor, who is primarily responsible for all government funds and property, enforce the settlement of disallowances in the amount of P1,532,583.96 within the prescribed period.

Further, the City Accountant should draw the necessary entries to adjust the unrecorded settlement of disallowances amounting to P4,200.00, unrecorded disallowance of P8,627.92 and erroneous adjustment amounting to P600.00, to establish the correct balance of the Receivable – Disallowances / Charges account.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office made an adjustment entry to adjust the unrecorded settlement & erroneous adjustment as per JEV#2023-09-009690. | Despite the issuance of demand letters by the City Legal Office to the concerned persons liable, the balance of Receivables-Disallowances/ Charges still amounted to P1,523,356.04 as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accounting Office to review the aforementioned accounts in order to determine with certainty the accountability of the former officials/personnel with regard the outstanding receivables and payables. Thereafter, demand letters should be issued to enforce the settlement of the same.

Henceforth, the City Accounting Office require retiring officials and employees to execute an affidavit of undertaking authorizing the deduction of accountabilities from their claims and by ensuring that all accountabilities are duly accounted for and considered in the computation and grant of their terminal leave benefits.
observations.managementAction:
The City Accounting Office is in the process of verification and reconciliation of the subsidiary ledger balances of the account. From our initial verification we have identified duplicate accounts for necessary adjustments.




The City Accounting Office already ensures that accountabilities are deducted from claims of officials and employees who are about to be separated from service with the City Government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accounting Office and the BAC Secretariat facilitate the submission of the duly approved Construction Safety and Health Program for the Maintenance of Roads along Caritan Highway, Caritan Centro, Tuguegarao City project and ensure that all infrastructure projects of the City are supported by the said program in strict compliance to Section 5 of DOLE Department Order No. 13 series of 1998.
observations.managementAction:
The contractor did not comply with the demand from the Office of the City Engineer to submit the duly approved Construction Safety and Health Program for the subject project.

The City Engineering Office, nonetheless, assured that they will deal with the matter accordingly and will strictly adhere to Section 5 of DOLE Department Order No. 13 series of 1998 in the infrastructure projects that they shall be implementing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the concerned offices and department to immediately implement the three (3) projects funded by DOST, PENRO and DOTr in the amount of P550,000.00, P510,000.00 and P4,000,000.00 respectively, in compliance with Section 16 of RA 7160.


Further, submit immediately the liquidation reports of the financial assistance for meals for Covid-19 to the Source Agencies LBP and MTWD in the amount of P820,000.00 and P800,000.00 respectively, and refund any unused amount if no longer needed for the purpose it was intended.
observations.managementAction:
Fund transfer from PENRO were implemented during the year and liquidation reports have been submitted.

Fund transfers from DOTR in the amount of P4,000,000.00 were returned due to mutual termination of the MOA.

Fund transfer from DOST remained unimplemented as of December 31, 2023.
observations.reason: No information was provided by Management to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor, together with the City Planning and Development Council, through the City Planning and Development Officer, ensure that the development projects for each budget year to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund (DF) are well planned and procurement-and-implementation ready to mitigate, if not totally prevent, delays in the implementation of projects pursuant to Section 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2022. Projects should be first judicially reviewed and assessed to determine any issues that will cause delay or non-implementation before these are budgeted in a given year. 

Moreover, we recommended that the City resolve issues in the implementation of the four (4) unimplemented projects in the amount of P97,810,000.00 to optimize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund as mandated under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
observations.managementAction:
Delays in the implementation of projects funded under the 20% Development Fund were still noted in CY 2023.




Based on the information gathered from the City Engineering Office, of the four (4) which were unimplemented in CY 2022, three (3) are still on-going and one (1) has been completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City DRRM Officer and other concerned implementing offices to fully utilize the available 70% Mitigation Fund and Continuing Appropriations fund and implement all budgeted projects, programs and activities in the amount of P25,583,030.18 to fully achieve its intended purpose in compliance with R.A. No. 10121.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2023, the utilization of the 70% Mitigation Fund, Continuing Appropriations and Special Trust Fund was only at 36.75%.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the City Accounting Office to:
Make the necessary adjustments, when necessary and upon receipt of information and result of verification from the concerned AGDBs, in compliance with Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 3.1, 3.2 and 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 so as to present fairly the balance of the Cash in Bank - LCCA account in the Statement of Financial Position as of year-end; and
Review and adjust the book error amounting to ₱690,000.00 which remained a reconciling item despite recording by the bank in CY 2022; elsewise, to exert earnest efforts to locate the source documents and examine the same for proper recording.

We recommended that the Management, through the City Accounting Office, review and recompute the provision of accumulated depreciation in the affected PPEs and make necessary adjustments in pursuance with IPSAS 17, and to fairly present the account balances of PPE and other related accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management:
through the Inventory Committee, complete the conduct of physical inventory of the City’s PPE and prepare and submit the corresponding report therefrom in strict compliance to Section 113 of Section 226 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs;
through the City Accounting Office, undertake continuous reconciliation on the result of the physical count with the property and accounting records and adjust the book of accounts and the property records, as warranted, to reflect the correct balances of the affected PPE accounts in order to fairly present the PPE accounts in the financial statements as of year-end; and
instruct the concerned offices to study the applicability of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 which provides the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE Items found at station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items, for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of government agencies to facilitate fair presentation of PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management, whenever practicable, (a) require the submission of liquidation reports, together with a copy of the journal entry voucher taking up the subject expenditures, from the concerned implementing agencies; and (b) refrain from providing additional financial assistance when the liquidation reports for previous grants were not yet submitted; in faithful adherence to Item 5 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 111(1) of PD No. 1445.
We further recommended that Management instruct the City Accounting Office to study the applicability of COA Circular No. 2023 – 008 dated August 17, 2023 which prescribes the guidelines and procedures to be observed for the proper disposition of dormant accounts.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the concerned departments to exert necessary efforts to locate all required documents for the titling of 18 sites which were acquired through donation or purchase by the City in order to establish its ownership over the subject lands and to protect the interest of the City; and
Constitute the Inventory Committee tasked to determine the value of the 37 lots with a total area of 193,313 square meters already titled in the name of the City pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to provide basis in recording the same in the books of accounts of the City by the City Accounting Office, to facilitate the fair presentation of the Land account in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly monitor the grant and enforce the settlement and liquidation of cash advances provided to officers and employees in strict compliance to applicable provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
When necessary, opt to withhold the salaries of officials and employees who failed to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of the same Circular; and
Through the City Legal Office, follow through with the issuance of demand letters to the concerned officials and employees or avail of other legal remedies to enforce the settlement or liquidation of cash advances granted.

We recommended that the Management, through the City Accounting Office, further analyze the unremitted balances in the total amount of ₱5.625 million in order to come-up with the details/composition thereof as basis in remitting to the concerned agencies in compliance with applicable rules and regulations.
Moreover, we recommended that the Management, through the City Accounting Office, continue exerting earnest efforts to determine the cause of the negative balances in the aforementioned payable accounts and make the necessary adjustments or corrections, whenever necessary.

We recommended that the Management enforce the settlement of disallowances in the amount of ₱1.524 million in strict compliance to Sections 5.4, 7.1.1 and 7.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2009 – 001 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that Management require the concerned NGO and the City Accounting Office or the focal persons/unit in charge of the project to submit the aforementioned lacking documentary requirements in conformity with Item 2.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, to enable the Audit Team to conduct proper evaluation of the said transaction.
We also recommended that Management strictly monitor the implementation of the projects and programs sourced from the said fund transfers and require the NGO to liquidate the same in compliance with Item 2.2 of the same Circular.

We recommended the Management, through the City Accounting Office and City Treasurer’s Office to cause the return of the unused balance of the fund transfers from various NGAs in compliance with Item 6.7 of COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and to record proper adjusting entries to facilitate fair presentation of the financial statements.
We further recommended that Management ensure the proper safekeeping of every MOA that the City will undertake in order to provide a proper basis for its monitoring of projects, programs and activities and to monitor compliance to various terms and condition agreed upon by the parties, including those pertaining to unutilized/unspent fund transfers.

We recommended that the Management create a composite team to work on the details/composition of the roads, highways and bridges previously recorded in the RPI which were already transferred to the Road Networks account in lump sum in the amount of ₱390.861 million as of December 31, 2023, to ensure that the booked amount reflects the correct value of the road networks.
Likewise, we recommended that the Management conduct physical count of road networks and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCLRN and update and maintain the Local Road Inventory Report and Road Map to ensure complete and accurate records of Road Network pursuant to Items VI (3) and VII (b) (1) of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We recommended that the Management:
Cease the grant of hazard pay to officials and employees who are no longer assigned in an establishment specifically mentioned in Section 21 of R.A. No. 7305 and are not principally engaged in the delivery of health or health-related services; and
Require the concerned official to refund the hazard pay granted to her from July 2022 to June 2023 amounting to P0.113 million.

We recommended that the BAC carefully perform detailed evaluation and post-qualification of eligible bids to ensure that computational errors and omissions are considered before the execution of the contract between the City and the winning bidder.
Likewise, we recommended that Management enforce the refund of the excess of the re-calculated bid price over the submitted financial bid proposal of the contractor amounting to P32,130.00, to protect the interest of the City.

We recommended that the BAC, moving forward, exercise diligence in the preparation and execution of contract agreements to prevent possible errors that may raise questions as to the validity and propriety of procurement documents and the processes they represent.
It is likewise recommended that the BAC ensure that the work duration stipulated in the contract is consistent with those stated in its accompanying documents in order to provide a valid basis for the monitoring of the City’s various projects and to properly determine delays and the resulting liquidated damages provided for under Section 8.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the BAC ensure that a representative of a duly recognized private group and an NGO be invited, in addition to representatives from the Commission on Audit, to sit as observers in the proceedings for all stages of procurement of the City’s various programs and projects, including the bid evaluation and post-qualification stages in faithful compliance to Section 13.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Management instruct the BAC to comply with the requirement on the submission of APPs, PMRs, and APCPI Results to the GPPB pursuant to applicable provisions of GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020, in order to enable the City to achieve its performance targets and to avert any risk of possible sanctions due to such non-compliance.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

The City Treasurer’s office to revisit its policies and strategies in order to improve the efficiency of the City’s collection of real property taxes;

The City Treasurer’s Office to enforce the collection of the RPT delinquencies in compliance with Sections 269 and 270 if RA 7160 in order to generate additional funds that it could use to support its socio-economic and development projects; and 

The City Accounting Office to draw the necessary adjusting entries to appropriately recognize the RPT and SET Receivables and the corresponding deferred income accounts amounting to Php 26,210,705.71 each.
observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant strictly monitor the settlement of unliquidated cash advances. Subsequently, officials and employees with outstanding cash advances who get separated from the service should be required to execute an Affidavit of Undertaking that their outstanding cash advances will be paid upon receipt of their terminal leave pay. And for those officers and employees still in the service, their failure to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause to withhold their salary.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive adopt strict measures to regulate the granting of cash advances in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and adhere to Item 6.0 of COA Circular 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012. Any unused or unspent cash advances should be returned or refunded. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: An amount of P6,222,428.58 in cash advances granted to officers and employees remain outstanding as of December 31, 2022.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Item 10, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that all required information pertaining to the terms of delivery in the Purchase Order be completely filled-out in order for the City General Services Office to properly monitor the implementation of the projects and to prevent the incurrence of delays in its completion. | AAR CY 2021

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accounting Office to assign specific personnel who shall be primarily responsible to conduct a thorough analysis of the subject payable accounts to determine the root cause of the negative balances, reconciling items and unaccounted transition balances in the total amount of ₱30,086,138.30, ₱298,904.68, and ₱6,341,058.12, respectively.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

the City Treasurer’s Office to revisit its policies and strategies in order to improve the efficiency of the City’s collection of real property taxes;

the City Treasurer’s Office to enforce the collection of the RPT delinquencies in compliance with Sections 269 and 270 of RA 7160 in order to generate additional funds that it could use to support its socio-economic and development projects; and

the City Accounting Office to draw the necessary adjusting entries to appropriately recognize the RPT and SET Receivables and the corresponding deferred income accounts amounting to ₱26,210,705.71 each.

Recommended Entry:
The City Treasury Office informed the Audit Team of the policies and strategies that the office instituted for the collection of real property taxes. However, despite its diligent efforts to advertise, publicize or make notice for the collection, he attributed the delinquency in the payment of taxes to the inability of the City to determine the correct addresses of the concerned taxpayers. The City Treasurer added that the office cannot enforce any legal remedies without sending first a notice to the taxpayer.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Health Office to conduct an inventory of the donated vaccines and submit a report on the disposition thereof to the City Accounting Office as the latter’s basis in recognizing the appropriate inventory and expense accounts for CY 2022.
Henceforth, the City Mayor should require all departments and offices to coordinate with the City Accounting Office for all donations received for proper recording and accounting thereof.

We recommended that:
The City Accounting Office draw the necessary adjusting entries to appropriately recognize the advance payments of Real Property Tax collections for CY 2023 collected in CY 2022 amounting to ₱1,891,531.41; and
Recommended Entry:
Henceforth, the City Treasury Office shall prepare and submit in a separate report all advance receipts of taxes from taxpayers to the City Accounting Office to facilitate proper accounting thereof in accordance with IPSAS 23 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Require the concerned departments to exert necessary efforts to locate all required documents for the titling of 18 sites which were acquired through donation or purchase by the City; and
Direct the Inventory Committee to determine the value of the 37 lots with a total area of 193,313 square meters already titled in the name of the City pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated 
January 31, 2020 in order to provide basis in recording the same in the books of accounts of the City by the City Accounting Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor create a task force committee that can responsibly and knowledgeably do the conduct of One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances as provided in the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.  If possible, assign three to five personnel from each division/office as members of the committee in order to facilitate the counting and determination of whereabouts of items located in their office.
The Property Officer in coordination with the committee, shall be responsible in the over-all preparation and submission of the required Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment as of December 31, 2022 and every year thereafter.
An Inventory Committee shall be constituted for the conduct of physical count and the preparation of the corresponding RPCPPE. Moreover, the Management was advised by the Audit Team and agreed to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts balances provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2022 in CY 2023.

We recommended that the City Mayor create a team to work on the details/composition of the roads, highways and bridges previously recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure (RPI) which were already transferred to the Road Networks account in lump sum amount of ₱355,720,730.21 as of December 31, 2022, to ensure that the booked amount reflects the correct value of the road networks.
Likewise, conduct physical count of Road Networks and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCLRN and update and maintain the Local Road Inventory Report and Road Map to ensure complete and accurate records of Road Network pursuant to Items VI (3) and VII (b) (1)   of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:

the City Treasurer’s Office to furnish the City Accounting Office the certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible from them using the updated Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card index card at the beginning of every year; and

the City Accounting Office to immediately draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the debit to Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and credit to Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income, based on the foregoing list.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accounting Office to prepare a list/schedule of PPE to be transferred to the City Schools Division Office/ Department of Education based on the existing subsidiary ledgers of the PPE accounts.
Thereafter, create an inventory team composed of City General Services Office and City Accounting Office, if possible, with a representative from the Schools Division Office/DepEd to conduct physical count to determine their existence as basis for the turn-over of the PPE.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to withhold the salaries of concerned officials and employees who failed to liquidate their cash advances at year-end pursuant to Items 5.1.3 and 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. Likewise, require the officials and employees with outstanding cash advances who were separated from the service to execute an Affidavit of Undertaking that their outstanding cash advances will be paid upon receipt of their terminal leave pay.
We further recommended that the City Mayor adopt strict measures to regulate the granting of cash advances in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accounting Office to assign one personnel to take charge in the preparation of the required documents for the request for write-off of dormant accounts in the total amount of ₱4,780,437.74 in accordance with applicable provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and immediately submit the same to COA for evaluation.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure the timely submission of any contracts entered into by the City for its various procurement projects within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.
The submission of the contracts and the accompanying supporting documents of the nine (9) procurement projects in the amount of ₱41,824,881.18 to the Audit Team is likewise enjoined.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office analyze the unremitted balances in the total amount of ₱17,128,484.85 in order to come-up with the details/composition thereof as basis in remitting to the concerned agencies.
Henceforth, all amounts withheld during the month should be immediately remitted in the ensuing month in faithful adherence to Section 69 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and other applicable rules and regulations. It should be emphasized that any penalty imposed against the City shall be the personal liability of the agency withholding agent.

We recommended that the City’s Procurement Unit and the Inspection and Acceptance Committee completely fill-up all required information in the Purchase Orders and Inspection and Acceptance Reports, respectively, to ensure propriety and validity of the transactions and serve as basis in the proper monitoring on the implementation of projects as well as the delivery of goods and services.
Likewise, the City Accounting Office should not process transactions with incomplete data on supporting documents to ensure complete information to prove the propriety and validity thereof.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City General Services Officer and City Accountant to discontinue the practice of paying and reimbursing fuel consumption which are not supported by proper and sufficient documentation in compliance with pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 to ensure propriety, legality and validity of the transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor, who is primarily responsible for all government funds and property, enforce the settlement of disallowances in the amount of ₱1,532,583.96 within the prescribed period.
Further, the City Accountant should draw the necessary journal entries to adjust the unrecorded settlement of disallowances amounting to ₱4,200.00 and erroneous adjustment amounting to ₱600.00, to establish the correct balance of the Receivables-Disallowances/Charges account.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
require the one hundred fifty-three (153) concerned job order personnel to refund the total amount of P520,528.86 representing the overpayment in their wages for July 2022. Otherwise, the transaction will be disallowed in audit;
instruct the OIC – Human Resource Management Officer to ensure that all job order personnel are immediately registered in the Electronic Time Card and require the use of attendance logbooks for proper control of their daily attendance in the office; and
direct the City Accounting Office to validate entries made in the daily time records against the attendance logbook before the processing of their claims for wages to ensure correct amount to be paid to the job order personnel

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accounting Office to review the aforementioned accounts in order to determine with certainty the accountability of the former officials/personnel with regard the outstanding receivables and payables with negative balances. Thereafter, demand letters should be issued to enforce settlement of the same.
Henceforth, the City Accounting Office require retiring officials and employees to execute an affidavit of undertaking authorizing the deduction of accountabilities from their claims and by ensuring that all accountabilities are duly accounted for and considered in the computation and grant of their TLB.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that performance securities posted by prospective bidders in procurement projects of the City conform to the forms and amounts prescribed under the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 to ensure that it is protected against possible failures of contractors to comply with the terms and obligations of their contracts.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office strictly comply with the provisions of BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 dated June 20, 2014 on the withholding of taxes from payments of goods and services purchased by the City in order to help the national government in generating revenues to meet its appropriation requirements and to avoid the imposition of penalties by the BIR.
The City Accounting Office is likewise enjoined to submit an explanation or justification as to why taxes were not withheld from the abovementioned payment of air fare and hotel accommodation.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working Group be cautious and exercise confidentiality of information whenever necessary in compliance with Section 19 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, in order to prevent doubts or questions from being raised on the propriety and validity of procurement processes being conducted.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee require contractors to place their signatures and the date of their receipt on the Notice to Proceed issued to them by the City for its various procurement programs in faithful compliance to Section 37.4.1 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended the City Mayor, thru the Human Resource Management Officer:
To prospectively include the specific duties and responsibilities of the job order personnel in their respective contracts as a guide in the assessment of the performance of their functions and accomplishment of their expected outputs; and
To require the job order personnel to quantify and/or provide details of the actual work they performed in their accomplishment reports on a per-day basis in order to properly and substantially support claims for their wages.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Assessor to undertake a general revision of real property assessments.
Once the revised assessment levels have been reviewed and refined, we recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod to approve the same through the enactment of an ordinance in conformity with Section 219 of Republic Act No. 7160.

We recommended the Petty Cash Custodian to regularly accomplish the certification of transactions at the end of each month as required under Section 90 (C)(2), Book II of the Local Treasury Operations Manual (LTOM).

We recommended and the City Treasurer’s Office agreed to limit the amount of cash advances to be granted to the Disbursing Officer within the maximum accountability stated in the bond policy in compliance to Item 5.1 of the Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that observers properly sign and indicate the date of their receipt in the invitation to attend the City’s various procurement activities in order to allow the Audit Team to determine whether the said invitation was issued in adherence to Section 13.3 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office and the BAC Secretariat facilitate the submission of the duly approved Construction Safety and Health Program for the Maintenance of Roads (Asphalt Overlay) along Caritan Highway, Caritan Centro, Tuguegarao City project and ensure that all infrastructure projects of the City are supported by the said program in strict compliance to Section 5 of DOLE Department Order No. 13 series of 1998.

We recommended that the City Mayor and City Vice Mayor coordinate with each other to provide the funding requirements to complete the unfinished works at the Tuguegarao City Commercial Center in order to generate possible additional revenues if the available space is rented in a commercial basis or to improve the rendition of services if the City decides to use it for administrative purposes. The completion of the third and fourth levels of the commercial center, leasing it out on commercial basis and conducting maintenance and beautification will not only protect the interest of the City, but will ultimately benefit its constituents in terms of better services. The proposal to seek private sector proponents to finish the Building under City Ordinance No. 052-2017 should also be reviewed and considered.

We recommended that Management instruct the concerned offices and department to immediately implement the three (3) projects funded by DOST, PENRO and DOTr in the amount of ₱550,000.00, ₱510,000.00 and ₱4,000,000.00, respectively, in compliance with Section 16 of RA No. 7160.
Further, submit immediately the liquidation reports of the financial assistance for meals for Covid-19 to the Source Agencies LBP and MTWD in the amount of ₱820,000.00 and ₱800,000.00, respectively, and refund any unused amount if no longer needed for the purpose it was intended.

We recommended that the City Mayor, together with the City Planning and Development Council, through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, judicially plan and evaluate the City’s PPAs for each budget year to properly identify priority development projects that can be implemented during the year through efficient planning and procurement activities to mitigate, if not totally prevent, delays in the implementation of projects pursuant to Section 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2022.
Moreover, we recommended the City to expedite the implementation of all unimplemented PPAs identified herein to optimize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund as mandated under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City DRRM Officer and other concerned implementing offices to fully utilize the available 70% Mitigation Fund and Continuing Appropriations fund and implement all budgeted projects, programs and activities in the amount of ₱25,583,030.18 to fully achieve its intended purpose in compliance with R.A No. 10121.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office regularly monitor the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statements by the personnel in-charge and determine causes of delay so that this can be addressed immediately, thus ensuring the timely preparation and submission of the said reports. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office submitted all the Bank Reconciliation Statement as of December 31, 2021 on March 14, 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant assign one personnel to prepare schedules/list of the infrastructure assets and to look into the source documents as basis in determining the details, description and useful life thereof which is needed in the computation of the depreciation. The accomplishment of the person assigned should be closely monitored in order to address any problems encountered in the delay in the computation and/or completion of the schedule of the depreciation.   It is stressed that the updated recording of the corresponding depreciation   will ensure fair presentation of the various infrastructure assets accounts in the financial statements. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office has already recorded the corresponding depreciation of the City’s various infrastructure asset accounts in the financial statements as of December 31, 2022.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management secure from the Tuguegarao City Peoples General Hospital the certified list of frontline workers who were accommodated in Villa Blanca Hotel to ensure validity and propriety on the payment of hotel accommodation in the amount of P3,194,531.25, and submit the same to COA for audit purposes.

Likewise, we requested the immediate submission of the required list of names and the statement of account to support the claim of D128 Lodge in the amount of P278,400.00.  Otherwise, these transactions shall be suspended in audit. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: The City General Service Office in coordination with the TCPGH submitted the list of names of frontline workers and the statement of account to support the claim of D128 Lodge and Villa Blanca Hotel.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Infrastructure Projects Unit submit immediately the plans/drawings for the four projects on COVID-19 PPAs (Repair and Maintenance of COVID facilities) with a total cost of P4,269,646.10 to ensure propriety and validity in the conduct of the procurement and likewise needed in the conduct of technical inspection thereof. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office already submitted the approved plans/drawings for the four projects on COVID-19 PPAs (Repairs and Maintenance of Covid Facilities).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that all the required information on lists of recipients or distribution lists for projects of the City that involve the distribution of goods purchased are completely filled-out and properly accomplished in order to prevent confusion as to the actual number of goods distributed and the remaining balance of goods to be recognized as an asset, thereby ensuring proper accounting thereof. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: In CY 2022, the City already provided sufficient information and details in the distribution lists of various goods for delivery to the City’s constituents.

The concerned contractor refunded the cost of Item VIII – Thermal Protection and Item XV – Roofing and Accessories for the Construction of Barangay Gymnasium at Annafunan West and Construction of Brgy. Gym Extension and Bleachers at Bagay projects, respectively.

The basis in the computation of liquidated damages is the project’s program of works which states that the contract duration shall be in working days, instead of calendar days. Hence, the actual liquidated damages deducted from the concerned contractor is correct and proper. 

On the other hand, the amount of P3,913.53 in liquidated damages has already been refunded by Fragger Construction.

The duly signed work suspension and resumption orders for the subject project have already been submitted to the Audit Team.




Management has not submitted documentary proof of repairs undertaken on the subject projects.


Delays in the completion of projects have so far not been noted by the Audit Team for CY 2022. Nonetheless, the Audit Team shall continue to monitor and check any delays in subsequent projects to be implemented and ensure that liquidated damages, if any, are deducted from contractors’ claims.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accounting Office ensure that all payments to be made for the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects through alternative modes are supported with the documentary requirements prescribed under applicable provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.


Further, we recommended that the lacking documents be submitted immediately to facilitate the review of the aforementioned transactions. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accounting Office submit the lacking documentary requirements to support various disbursements on the payment of advertising expenses   amounting to P1,217,500.00 to ensure propriety and validity of the transactions in compliance with Items G, L(1), L(2) and Item II of Appendix A of Annex H of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184. Otherwise, these will be suspended in audit. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: Payments for the City’s advertising expenses are now supported by documentary requirements prescribed under Items G, L(1), L(2) and Item II of Appendix A of Annex H of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

observations.managementAction: The Head of the Infrastructure Projects Unit informed the Audit Team that the revised contract costs are within the total appropriations for the subject procurement projects as indicated in the Certification on the Availability of Funds issued by the City Accounting Office.

observations.managementAction: Review of procurement projects entered into by the City disclosed that the Bids and Awards Committee already exercises care and caution in the conduct of various procurement processes, thereby preventing the incurrence of the same observation.

observations.managementAction:

The concerned contractors have so far refunded a total amount of P37,133.13. The excess in the contract cost amounting to P8,307.60 on the Construction of Masonry Drainage Structure, Magora Street, Libag Norte, Tuguegarao City project has yet to be refunded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that variation orders issued by Management be within the threshold prescribed under Section 1.1 of Annex E of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 and that an explanation or justification be submitted for the excess of the amount of variation order over the prescribed percentage of the contract price. | AAR CY 2021
observations.managementAction: An amount of P2,275.69 representing the excess of the approved variation order over the allowed percentage of the contract price has been refunded by the concerned contractor.
